Analysis.description: Describe yourself: What do you teach? Who are your students?
Identify a lesson that could be more engaging
List 3 things your students struggle with

Design.description: Sketch the learning journey in 3 moments: hook — practice — aha
Write one measurable objective using a Bloom's verb (e.g. 'students will identify...')
Choose your delivery format: game, storyboard, simulation, or lesson plan

Development.description: Draft the opening 60 seconds of your experience (the hook)
Build one practice activity that lets learners attempt the skill
Write the feedback loop: what does the learner see when they fail? When they succeed?

Implementation.description: Run through your draft experience yourself — time it
Identify one moment of friction (confusing, slow, unclear)
Fix the friction and implement the corrected version

Evaluation.description: Define success: what metric proves this experience worked?
Compare your original 'Ordinary World' description to what you built
Write one sentence: 'I know this works when I see a learner...'

Contrast.description: Find a bad example of teaching your subject — name exactly what makes it forgettable
Find a great example — name the one thing that makes it stick
List how your design differs from both: what is your Contrast principle?

Repetition.description: Identify the ONE core concept that must be encountered multiple times
Design 3 different contexts where learners meet that concept (Pythagorean breadth)
Assign each encounter to a different sense or modality (see / do / explain)

Alignment.description: Check: does your hook connect directly to your measurable objective?
Check: does your practice activity match the verb in your objective (identify, build, compare...)?
Check: does your success metric match what the activity actually produces?

Proximity.description: Cluster related content together — what belongs in Act 1 vs Act 2 vs Act 3?
Remove one thing that doesn't belong in Ch 1's scope
Draw (or describe) the spatial layout of your experience: where does the learner look first?

Envision.description: Write your PEARL Vision statement: 'When this works, the learner will feel...'
Describe the emotional arc: bored/confused → engaged → capable → proud
Ask Pete: 'Does my experience create this arc?' — log Pete's Socratic response

Yoke.description: Connect your learning objective to a real-world moment the student will face
Name the stakeholder who benefits most when the student masters this (parent, employer, community)
Yoke the abstract concept to a concrete, memorable metaphor — write it in one sentence

Evolve.description: Compare Ch 1's PEARL to what you imagined when you started — what changed?
Write the opening paragraph of your LitRPG chapter: 'In the Ordinary World, the teacher saw...'
Commit the Ch 1 design to your Book — the Iron Road continues to Ch 2

Analysis.description: Research how AI could transform this learning experience
List 3 'impossible' things AI now makes possible for your subject
Draft your Call: 'What if learning could be...?'

Design.description: Design how the AI party member serves this lesson (Pete? The Visionary? The Engineer?)
Write 3 prompts you would ask the AI to unlock this learning
Prototype the AI interaction loop: student input → AI response → student action

Development.description: Build the AI prompt template for this lesson's core activity
Test the prompt with 3 different student starting points
Refine the prompt based on the worst response you received

Implementation.description: Integrate the AI interaction into your Ch 1 prototype
Record one full student-AI exchange — what surprised you?
Identify where learners trusted the AI vs where they pushed back

Evaluation.description: Did the AI interaction increase or decrease student confidence? Evidence?
What was the most unexpected AI output — and what does it teach you about your design?
Update your PEARL Vision: has the Call changed what success looks like?

Contrast.description: Compare your AI-enhanced lesson to the original non-AI version — what fundamentally changed?
Find one thing AI does worse than a human teacher for this lesson — name it honestly
Define your Contrast principle: where does AI amplify, and where must the human lead?

Repetition.description: Identify the core AI interaction pattern learners will repeat throughout the experience
Design 3 variations of the AI prompt that spiral in complexity (Remember → Apply → Create)
Ensure each variation reinforces the same learning objective from a different angle

Alignment.description: Check: does the AI prompt directly serve your measurable learning objective?
Check: does the AI response format match how you'll assess understanding?
Check: is the AI interaction necessary, or could a simpler method achieve the same goal?

Proximity.description: Group your AI interactions by complexity — which comes first, second, third?
Place the AI 'call to adventure' moment within the first 3 minutes of the experience
Ensure scaffolding (instructions, examples) appears immediately before the AI interaction

Envision.description: Envision the learner's face when the AI responds to their first real question — describe it
Describe the moment a skeptical teacher sees this working — what converts them?
Write the one-line pitch: 'This changes learning because...'

Yoke.description: Connect the AI interaction to a skill students will use in their career, not just the classroom
Name one real-world professional who uses this exact AI workflow today
Yoke the 'Call to Adventure' to a story: 'Once, a teacher discovered that AI could...'

Evolve.description: How has your understanding of AI in education evolved since Ch 1?
Write your Ch 2 LitRPG paragraph: 'The Call came when the teacher realized...'
Commit Ch 2 to your Book — the locomotive gains speed

Analysis.description: List your fears: 'I can't because...' (be honest, no filter)
Identify your first Creep — what did you add that wasn't in the PEARL?
Apply the Coal-Steam principle: pick ONE 20-minute win that builds momentum

Design.description: Design a 'minimum viable lesson' — what is the simplest version that still works?
Cut any feature that requires more than 20 minutes of new learning to implement
Document what you're NOT building — the Nope List

Development.description: Build only the minimum viable lesson you designed
Time the development — track coal burned vs steam generated
Note every moment you wanted to add something extra — log them as future Creeps

Implementation.description: Deploy the minimum viable lesson to one real learner
Observe without explaining — watch where they get stuck
Record the timestamp of their first 'aha' moment

Evaluation.description: Did the minimum viable lesson produce the learning outcome? Yes/No — why?
Review your Creep log — which ones were actually worthwhile?
Apply the Heavilon Algorithm to one failure: 'one brick higher' — what's the next iteration?

Contrast.description: Compare your full dream design to your minimum viable lesson — what did you lose? What remained?
Name the one feature you refused that hurt the most to cut
Ask: is the thing you cut a Creep, or a genuine requirement? How do you tell the difference?

Repetition.description: Identify which simplification you'll revisit in a future chapter (planned repetition)
Design a 'breadcrumb trail': 3 micro-moments in the MVP that foreshadow deeper content later
Repeat the core concept in the MVP using two different modalities (visual + verbal, or kinesthetic + written)

Alignment.description: Check: does the MVP still align with the Bloom's verb in your original objective?
Check: did cutting features accidentally remove the assessment mechanism?
Check: is the feedback loop still intact in the simplified version?

Proximity.description: In your MVP, is the instruction physically close to the practice? (Don't make learners scroll/hunt)
Group the essential content into one screen or one page — no navigation required
Place the 'aha moment' within 5 minutes of the learner starting — proximity to engagement

Envision.description: Envision your MVP working perfectly — what does the learner say when they finish?
Describe the worst case: what happens if the MVP fails? Is that survivable?
Write a 'Refusal Overcome' statement: 'I was afraid of X, but I built Y anyway because...'

Yoke.description: Connect your fear to a universal teaching fear — name it ('I'm not technical enough', 'students won't engage')
Yoke the Refusal to a student experience: 'My students refuse to try X because...' — how is that similar?
Name one colleague who shares this fear — how would you coach them through it?

Evolve.description: How has your relationship with scope changed since Ch 1? Are you better at saying no?
Write your Ch 3 LitRPG paragraph: 'The Yardmaster hesitated at the junction, but...'
Commit Ch 3 to your Book — the Refusal is behind you

Analysis.description: Read the ADDIE framework documentation — note 3 things that surprise you
Map your current problem to all 5 ADDIE phases — where are you now?
Choose your AI Party roles: Conductor, Engineer, Artist, Evaluator

Design.description: Design the learning relationship: how will Pete guide without telling?
Write 3 Socratic questions Pete should ask at this phase
Map which AI party member leads each ADDIECRAPEYE station

Development.description: Build the first Pete interaction in your experience (the mentor moment)
Write the mentor's opening question — it must reframe the learner's current belief
Create the 'Mentor's Tool' the learner receives: a framework, a checklist, a metaphor

Implementation.description: Test the mentor interaction with a learner — did they feel guided or lectured?
Log the exact moment the learner shifted from passive to active
Refine Pete's question based on what actually caused the shift

Evaluation.description: Did the AI mentor produce the 'I never thought of it that way' moment?
What would a mentor who OVER-explains look like? How is yours different?
Update PEARL: does the mentor's guidance align with the original Vision?

Contrast.description: Compare Pete-as-mentor to a traditional textbook explanation of the same concept
Compare Pete-as-mentor to a YouTube tutorial — what does Socratic dialogue add?
Define the Contrast: 'A mentor asks, a teacher tells, Pete does...'

Repetition.description: Identify 3 moments in the experience where the mentor's core question should resurface
Design the question spiral: same question, deeper context each time
Ensure the mentor's metaphor/framework appears in at least 2 different activities

Alignment.description: Check: does the mentor's guidance lead directly to the next learning objective?
Check: does the Socratic question match the Bloom's level of the current phase?
Check: can the learner proceed without the mentor? (Good mentoring makes itself unnecessary)

Proximity.description: Place the mentor's key insight immediately before the learner's hardest challenge
Ensure the 'Mentor's Tool' is accessible during (not just before) the practice activity
Group mentor guidance with worked examples — no orphaned advice

Envision.description: Envision a learner who has fully internalized the mentor's lesson — how do they teach it to someone else?
Describe the moment Pete's question unlocks genuine understanding — what does that look like?
Write: 'The mentor's gift was not an answer, but the question...'

Yoke.description: Connect the mentor's lesson to a mentor from your own life — what did they teach you by asking?
Yoke the learning framework to a physical metaphor learners can hold onto (map, compass, mirror)
Name the real-world skill this mentoring pattern prepares the learner for

Evolve.description: How has your understanding of Socratic teaching evolved since Ch 1?
Write your Ch 4 LitRPG paragraph: 'The Mentor appeared at the crossroads and asked...'
Commit Ch 4 to your Book — the Mentor's gift is yours to carry

Analysis.description: Write 3 learner personas: who will encounter this experience in the real world?
Define the entry prerequisite: what must learners already know to start?
Set your 'coal budget' — realistic time per week you can commit to this build

Design.description: Design the onboarding: how does a learner cross the threshold in your experience?
Write the 'point of no return' moment — when the learner is committed to the journey
Design the early win: what can a learner succeed at in the first 5 minutes?

Development.description: Build the onboarding sequence (first 5 minutes of the experience)
Implement the early win — make it achievable but not trivial
Test: does the early win teach the concept or just feel good?

Implementation.description: Deploy the onboarding to a first-time learner — observe without coaching
Measure time-to-first-success — if > 7 minutes, redesign
Log the exact words learners use to describe the experience after onboarding

Evaluation.description: Did learners cross the threshold with confidence or confusion?
Compare observed behavior to your 3 personas — which persona struggled most?
Revise the threshold for the struggling persona — what one change helps them most?

Contrast.description: Compare your onboarding to a popular app's onboarding (Duolingo, Khan Academy) — what do they do differently?
Compare the experience of your strongest persona vs your weakest — where does the gap appear?
Define your threshold Contrast: 'Before this moment, the learner believed X. After, they know Y.'

Repetition.description: Identify the core mechanic learners will repeat 10+ times throughout the full experience
Introduce that mechanic in its simplest form during onboarding — no penalties, just practice
Design 3 escalating versions of the mechanic that appear in later chapters

Alignment.description: Check: does the onboarding teach skills the learner will actually use in later phases?
Check: does the early win connect to the final assessment (not just fun for fun's sake)?
Check: do all 3 personas have a viable path through the threshold?

Proximity.description: Place the 'point of no return' exactly at the moment of highest engagement
Ensure instructions and the first interactive element are on the same screen
Group prerequisite knowledge check immediately before the threshold — not 3 screens earlier

Envision.description: Envision 100 learners crossing this threshold simultaneously — does it scale?
Describe the emotion you want a learner to feel 30 seconds after crossing: curiosity? determination? play?
Write: 'The threshold is not a door — it is a mirror that shows the learner they are ready'

Yoke.description: Connect the threshold to the learner's daily life: 'You already crossed a threshold today when you...'
Yoke the onboarding to game design wisdom: 'World 1-1 teaches you to jump by making you need to jump'
Name the real-world threshold this prepares learners for (first job, first presentation, first code deploy)

Evolve.description: How has your understanding of onboarding evolved since Ch 1?
Write your Ch 5 LitRPG paragraph: 'The Yardmaster crossed the threshold and the tracks stretched into...'
Commit Ch 5 to your Book — the adventure has truly begun

Analysis.description: Activate each AI party member (Pete, Visionary, Engineer, Brakeman) for their specialty
Complete your first micro-learning prototype in 20 minutes exactly
Defeat a Creep by applying the Heavilon Algorithm: name it, contain it, use it

Design.description: Design the 'test' sequence: 3 escalating challenges that build on each other
Assign an AI ally to each challenge: who coaches what?
Write the 'enemy' for this lesson: the misconception learners must defeat

Development.description: Build the 3-challenge sequence with escalating difficulty
Build the misconception encounter — make the wrong answer attractive, not obvious
Wire the AI ally response for each challenge outcome

Implementation.description: Run 3 learners through the challenge sequence — log failure points
Which enemy (misconception) survived? Did learners defeat it or carry it forward?
Identify which AI ally was most effective — and why

Evaluation.description: Did the escalating challenges create flow (challenge matched ability)?
Did the misconception get corrected permanently or temporarily?
Rate your AI party: who was an ally, who was still an enemy to the design?

Contrast.description: Compare your hardest challenge to your easiest — is the difficulty curve smooth or jarring?
Compare the misconception encounter to a traditional 'wrong answer' feedback — what makes yours stick?
Contrast the AI ally's response to a human tutor's — where does each excel?

Repetition.description: Ensure the misconception is encountered at least twice — once to fail, once to correct
Design a callback: the hardest concept from Ch 3 reappears in a new context here
Repeat the core skill 3 times with increasing autonomy (guided → supported → independent)

Alignment.description: Check: does each challenge explicitly map to a learning objective?
Check: is the misconception a real student error, or something you invented?
Check: do the AI ally responses scaffold without giving away the answer?

Proximity.description: Place each AI ally's help adjacent to the challenge it supports — not behind a menu
Group related challenges on the same screen or in the same scene
Ensure the misconception correction appears immediately after the learner encounters the error

Envision.description: Envision a learner who defeats the misconception — how do they explain it to a friend?
Describe the 'ally moment': when does the AI genuinely help, not just display information?
Write: 'The test was not about getting it right — it was about understanding why it was wrong'

Yoke.description: Connect the misconception to a real-world mistake professionals make in this field
Yoke the ally system to collaboration: 'In your career, you will need to ask for help from...'
Name the test your learner will face outside of school that this prepares them for

Evolve.description: How has your understanding of challenge design evolved since Ch 1?
Write your Ch 6 LitRPG paragraph: 'The first real test came when...'
Commit Ch 6 to your Book — allies gathered, enemies identified

Analysis.description: Map learning objectives to Bloom's taxonomy: are you reaching Create?
Design the assessment rubric using Quality Matters standards
Create a learner journey map through the complete experience

Design.description: Design the 'cave entrance' — the hardest concept in your lesson
Write the scaffold: what preparation did the learner receive to attempt this?
Design the 'backup path': what does a learner who fails do next?

Development.description: Build the hardest activity in the experience
Build the scaffold (hints, partial examples, worked models)
Build the recovery path — failure is data, not a wall

Implementation.description: Observe 3 learners attempting the hardest activity — do NOT help
Log every scaffold used — which ones were invisible (best kind)?
Track how many learners took the recovery path and whether it worked

Evaluation.description: Was the hardest moment hard enough? (Too easy = no growth, too hard = abandonment)
Did the scaffold fade appropriately as learners gained confidence?
Update PEARL: does the cave depth match the Vision you set in Ch 1?

Contrast.description: Compare your hardest activity to the easiest one — is the distance intentional?
Compare your scaffold to hand-holding: where is the line between support and crutch?
Contrast a learner who took the recovery path with one who didn't need it — what's different?

Repetition.description: Ensure the scaffold introduces concepts the learner has already seen in simpler form
Design the 'cave echo': the hardest concept reappears in Ch 9 (The Reward) in a triumphant context
Repeat the core skill under pressure — can the learner perform it with a time constraint or added complexity?

Alignment.description: Check: does the hardest activity directly assess the highest Bloom's level in your PEARL?
Check: does the recovery path lead back to the same learning objective (not a shortcut around it)?
Check: does the rubric match what the activity actually produces?

Proximity.description: Place the scaffold immediately before the cave entrance — not 3 lessons earlier
Ensure the recovery path is visible (learners must know it exists before they fail)
Group all hints and worked examples in a collapsible 'helper' panel next to the challenge

Envision.description: Envision a learner emerging from the cave — what has changed about them?
Describe the moment of deepest struggle: is it productive struggle or destructive frustration?
Write: 'The cave teaches what the sunlight cannot — that you are stronger than you believed'

Yoke.description: Connect the cave challenge to the hardest real-world application of this knowledge
Yoke the recovery path to resilience: 'In every career, you will fail at the hardest thing first'
Name the professional scenario where this exact struggle pattern appears

Evolve.description: How has your understanding of productive struggle evolved since Ch 1?
Write your Ch 7 LitRPG paragraph: 'The cave entrance loomed, and the teacher thought...'
Commit Ch 7 to your Book — the deepest challenge awaits

Analysis.description: Generate the Bevy project scaffold with the Engineer party member
Build the core game loop (ECS systems: update → render → input)
Create at least one interactive learning activity inside the game

Design.description: Design the game's win condition — how does the learner 'complete' the lesson?
Design the loss condition — what does failure look like, and what does it teach?
Map each game mechanic to a learning objective (no mechanic without pedagogy)

Development.description: Implement the win state with a clear success message that references the learning
Implement the loss state with a redirect (not a punishment)
Implement one adaptive element: the game responds to player behavior

Implementation.description: Run a 30-minute live playtest with a real learner — record it if possible
Track completion rate and average time to win condition
Find the one mechanic learners ignored — why did they skip it?

Evaluation.description: Did the Ordeal (hardest game moment) produce the intended learning?
Which mechanic created the most Bloom's-level thinking (Create/Evaluate)?
Is this a game that teaches, or content disguised as a game? Be honest.

Contrast.description: Compare your game to a worksheet covering the same content — what does the game add?
Compare your win/loss conditions to a standard quiz pass/fail — is yours more meaningful?
Contrast a learner playing your game vs reading about your subject — which produces deeper understanding?

Repetition.description: Ensure the core learning mechanic repeats at least 5 times with increasing stakes
Design a 'death and retry' loop where each failure teaches something the next attempt uses
Revisit the mentor's lesson from Ch 4 — does the game embody what Pete taught?

Alignment.description: Check: does every game mechanic serve a learning objective (remove any that are just 'fun filler')?
Check: does the adaptive element respond to understanding, not just performance?
Check: can you assess learning from gameplay data alone, without a separate test?

Proximity.description: Place learning feedback within the game world — not in a popup that breaks immersion
Ensure the 'Ordeal moment' occurs in the middle of the experience, not at the end
Group related game mechanics spatially — things that work together should be near each other

Envision.description: Envision a learner who beats the game — do they understand the subject, or just the game?
Describe the Ordeal moment from the learner's perspective — is it thrilling or frustrating?
Write: 'The Ordeal strips away pretense and asks: do you truly understand, or were you just following instructions?'

Yoke.description: Connect the game's core mechanic to a real-world application of the subject
Yoke the Ordeal to a career moment: 'The first time you do this for real, it will feel like...'
Name the transfer: what skill from the game directly applies to life outside it?

Evolve.description: How has your understanding of 'game-based learning' evolved since Ch 1?
Write your Ch 8 LitRPG paragraph: 'The Ordeal demanded everything, and the Yardmaster gave...'
Commit Ch 8 to your Book — you survived the Ordeal

Analysis.description: Compile and run your complete experience from start to finish
Play through the entire experience yourself — no skipping
Document what you built: title, audience, objective, delivery format, time to complete

Design.description: Design the celebration moment — how does the learner know they've earned the Reward?
Design the reflection prompt: 'What did you build? What does it prove about you?'
Design the 'call to teach' — how will this learner share what they learned?

Development.description: Build the celebration/completion experience (badge, certificate, narrative climax)
Build the reflection journal page or summary artifact
Build the share mechanism: export, publish, or print

Implementation.description: Let a learner experience the full end-to-end journey for the first time
Capture their exact words when they reach the Reward — quote them
Share the experience with one other educator — get their reaction

Evaluation.description: Did the Reward feel earned, or just appear? What's the difference you designed?
Does the learner leave with a tangible artifact they can use in the real world?
Measure transfer: one week later, can the learner apply the skill?

Contrast.description: Compare your Reward to a generic 'Congratulations!' screen — what makes yours meaningful?
Contrast the learner at the start vs the learner at the Reward — name 3 specific changes
Compare your reflection prompt to a standard course evaluation — which produces genuine insight?

Repetition.description: Does the Reward reference the same core concept from Ch 1, now fully mastered?
Design a callback: show the learner their first attempt next to their final attempt
Repeat the mentor's question from Ch 4 — can the learner now answer it themselves?

Alignment.description: Check: does the Reward directly celebrate achieving the measurable learning objective?
Check: does the reflection artifact demonstrate Bloom's Create or Evaluate level thinking?
Check: can a third party verify learning from the artifact alone?

Proximity.description: Place the Reward immediately after the final challenge — no gap between effort and recognition
Ensure the reflection prompt appears while the experience is fresh (same session)
Group the celebration, reflection, and share mechanisms on one page or one flow

Envision.description: Envision 1000 learners completing this — what does that portfolio of artifacts look like?
Describe the learner showing their Reward to someone who matters to them — a parent, a boss, a student
Write: 'The Reward is not the certificate — it is the person who earned it'

Yoke.description: Connect the artifact to a real portfolio the learner can use (job application, teaching demo, publication)
Yoke the Reward to professional credentialing: 'This artifact proves you can...'
Name one door this Reward opens that was closed before the learner started

Evolve.description: How has your definition of 'reward' in education evolved since Ch 1?
Write your Ch 9 LitRPG paragraph: 'The Reward gleamed in the furnace — not gold, but proof of...'
Commit Ch 9 to your Book — the treasure is real

Analysis.description: Conduct a pilot test with 3 real learners (not colleagues — real target audience)
Collect feedback using the Quality Matters rubric dimensions
Identify the top 3 improvements needed — rank by learner impact

Design.description: Redesign the one moment with the lowest completion rate
Design an A/B test: original vs. revised version of that moment
Write the iteration brief: what hypothesis are you testing?

Development.description: Build the revised version based on pilot feedback
Document every change made and why (your personal changelog)
Run the A/B version with 2 learners — one sees each version

Implementation.description: Deploy the iteration to a broader group (5-10 learners if available)
Track the delta: did completion rate improve? Did the aha moments move earlier?
Identify the next iteration — the Road Back never truly ends

Evaluation.description: Compare V1 to V2 with data: what changed and by how much?
Did the iteration improve learning, or just improve satisfaction?
Is this experience ready to publish — or does it need Ch 11's polish?

Contrast.description: Compare V1 and V2 side by side — name the single most impactful change
Compare your iteration process to 'just shipping it' — what would you have missed?
Contrast pilot feedback from a learner who loved it vs one who struggled — who taught you more?

Repetition.description: Identify which core concept was still misunderstood after V1 — add one more encounter in V2
Design a spaced repetition check: does the learner retain the concept 48 hours later?
Revisit the onboarding from Ch 5 — does the V2 experience still honor that first impression?

Alignment.description: Check: did the revision maintain alignment with the original learning objective?
Check: did the A/B test measure learning, not just preference?
Check: does the revised experience still match the PEARL Vision from Ch 1?

Proximity.description: In the revised version, place help closer to the point of confusion (not in a FAQ)
Group iteration notes with the specific screen or moment they apply to
Ensure feedback collection happens during the experience, not only after

Envision.description: Envision V3 — if you had unlimited time, what would the perfect version look like?
Describe the Road Back as a teacher: 'Iteration is not failure, it is...'
Write: 'The Road Back teaches that every version is a draft — even the 'final' one'

Yoke.description: Connect the iteration process to professional development: 'Every lesson plan you'll ever write will be revised'
Yoke A/B testing to the scientific method — your classroom is your laboratory
Name an industry outside education that iterates this way (software, medicine, engineering)

Evolve.description: How has your tolerance for imperfection evolved since Ch 1?
Write your Ch 10 LitRPG paragraph: 'The Road Back was not a retreat — it was a return with sharper eyes...'
Commit Ch 10 to your Book — the iteration strengthens the rail

Analysis.description: Apply WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards — run an automated check
Pass the Quality Matters rubric review (8 standards, 41 specific review standards)
Write the game's documentation and teacher implementation guide

Design.description: Design the mobile/tablet experience — does it work without a mouse?
Design for low-bandwidth: can a learner access this on a 3G connection?
Design the educator version: what does a teacher see that learners don't?

Development.description: Implement keyboard navigation for all interactive elements
Add alt text / ARIA labels to all non-text content
Build the educator dashboard: learner progress, time-on-task, completion status

Implementation.description: Test with one learner who uses assistive technology (screen reader, voice control)
Test the educator dashboard with one teacher — ask them to find a specific learner's data
Run the complete Quality Matters self-review — score each standard honestly

Evaluation.description: Did accessibility improvements break anything for non-assistive-tech users?
Quality Matters score: how many standards are fully met? Set a ship threshold (≥ 85%)
Is this experience ready to represent your teaching practice publicly?

Contrast.description: Compare the accessible version to the original — did accessibility improve the design for everyone?
Compare your educator dashboard to a standard LMS gradebook — what insights does yours add?
Contrast 'accessible' vs 'universal design': which are you building, and does it matter?

Repetition.description: Verify that all repeated interactions (buttons, forms, navigation) are consistent across the entire experience
Ensure the teacher implementation guide references the same vocabulary used in the learner experience
Check: does the final experience still repeat the core concept at the frequency you designed in Ch 1?

Alignment.description: Check: does the educator dashboard data map directly to the learning objectives?
Check: do WCAG improvements align with your learner personas' needs?
Check: does the teacher guide explain WHY each design choice serves the pedagogy?

Proximity.description: Place ARIA labels on the interactive elements themselves, not in a separate accessibility section
Ensure the educator dashboard shows data in context — next to the activity it measures
Group the implementation guide by lesson flow, not by technical feature

Envision.description: Envision a learner with a disability completing your experience independently — what does that mean?
Describe a teacher who has never used your tool opening it for the first time — can they figure it out?
Write: 'The Resurrection proves the experience can survive any context — any device, any learner, any teacher'

Yoke.description: Connect accessibility to equity: 'Every learner who can't access this is a learner we failed'
Yoke Quality Matters to professional credibility: this is the standard your peers will judge you by
Name the regulation or policy that requires this level of accessibility in your context

Evolve.description: How has your understanding of 'quality' evolved since Ch 1? Is it just 'works' or something deeper?
Write your Ch 11 LitRPG paragraph: 'The Resurrection tested not the idea, but the Yardmaster who built it...'
Commit Ch 11 to your Book — the final polish reveals the gem

Analysis.description: Package the experience as a distributable file (zip, crate, SCORM, or web link)
Upload to consciousframework.com or your chosen platform
Write your LitRPG dev novel chapter for greatrecycler.com — the story of this build

Design.description: Design the 'elixir': what reusable template, framework, or insight does this produce?
Design the handoff: what does the next educator need to run this without you?
Write the 1-page summary: problem → solution → evidence → replication instructions

Development.description: Build the replication package (all assets, source, data, and documentation)
Record a 5-minute demo walkthrough video for the next Yardmaster
Write the metadata: subject, audience, duration, prerequisites, Bloom's level

Implementation.description: Publish to at least one channel outside your own context (LMS, GitHub, community)
Present the experience to one peer — watch them use your handoff documentation
Submit to one external review: a colleague, a journal, a conference, or a community

Evaluation.description: Could another educator reproduce this experience from your documentation alone?
Look at your original PEARL from Ch 1 — did you build what you envisioned?
Close the circle: what is YOUR aha moment from building this? Write it as the Elixir.

Contrast.description: Compare your final product to your Ch 1 Ordinary World description — how far have you come?
Compare your elixir to a standard lesson plan template — what does yours offer that templates don't?
Contrast who you were as a designer in Ch 1 vs who you are now — name the growth

Repetition.description: Revisit every PEARL Vision statement from Ch 1-11 — did the thread hold across all chapters?
Identify the one lesson you learned that repeated in every chapter (your meta-insight)
Design: how will learners who complete your experience repeat this process for their own teaching?

Alignment.description: Final alignment check: does the published experience match every measurable objective from Ch 1?
Check: does the replication package include assessment evidence, not just activities?
Check: would Quality Matters certify this as meeting all applicable standards?

Proximity.description: In the replication package, place the 'quick start' guide on page 1 (not buried after theory)
Ensure the demo video shows the same experience the documentation describes
Group the Elixir (your reusable insight) with concrete examples of how to apply it

Envision.description: Envision this experience being used 5 years from now — what would need to change?
Describe the next Yardmaster who picks up your Elixir — what do they build with it?
Write: 'The Elixir is not the experience I built — it is the understanding I gained by building it'

Yoke.description: Connect your Elixir to the broader field: how does this contribute to education, not just your classroom?
Yoke the Return to professional identity: 'I am not just a teacher — I am a designer of learning'
Name the one thing you will do differently in your next design because of this journey

Evolve.description: Stand at the end of the Iron Road and look back — what did the journey teach you about yourself?
Write your final LitRPG paragraph: 'The Yardmaster returned with the Elixir — not a potion, but a promise...'
Commit Ch 12 to your Book — close the circle. The Iron Road is complete.
